title: SEGMENT 5 — Phages as the original substrate of molecular biology

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Phages as the original substrate of molecular biology

Now we leave the therapy thread for a while — we'll come back to it — and we follow the other thread, the one where phages became the experimental substrate on which essentially every fundamental concept of molecular biology was first established.
If you want to understand why the 1940s and 1950s biologists were obsessed with phage, you have to understand what they were trying to do and why phage was the perfect tool for it.
The question, in the 1930s and 1940s, was: what is a gene, made of?
What carries genetic information?
Cells, they reasoned, were too complicated.
A bacterial cell, even a stripped-down one like E. coli, has thousands of genes, dozens of metabolic pathways, an internal architecture you could barely characterize.
If you wanted to ask the most basic question — what molecule carries heredity — you needed the simplest possible genetic system.
And phages, it turned out, were the simplest genetic systems available.
A phage is essentially a tiny package of nucleic acid wrapped in protein.
Infect a bacterium, the phage's hereditary material directs the production of hundreds of new phages, and those progeny carry the same heritable traits.
It's heredity reduced to its absolute minimum.

In 1940, a group of physicists-turned-biologists — most notably Max Delbrück, a German physicist who'd emigrated to the United States, and Salvador Luria — formed what came to be called the phage group.

They met every summer at Cold Spring Harbor on Long Island, ran courses, taught each other the techniques, agreed on which phages they would standardize on — the so-called T-even phages, T2, T4, and T6, infecting E. coli — and built a community around the idea that if you wanted to understand life at the molecular level, you should work on phage.
Delbrück was famous for insisting on rigor — he came from physics, he was used to clean quantitative experiments — and his influence shaped the field's culture.
The phage group included almost everyone who would go on to win Nobel Prizes for foundational molecular biology in the 1950s and 60s.

The single most famous experiment to come out of this tradition is the Hershey-Chase experiment of 1952, and it's worth dwelling on because it's the experiment that proved, beyond reasonable doubt, that DNA is the genetic material.

Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase, working at Cold Spring Harbor, designed the experiment around T2 phage and the fact that, when T2 infects an E. coli cell, only the genetic material enters the cell — the protein shell stays outside, stuck to the surface like a discarded delivery package, as we mentioned in Segment 2.

So if you could label the DNA and the protein with distinguishable tags, you could ask: which one goes inside the cell, and therefore which one carries the heritable instructions for making new phages?
They did this with radioactive isotopes.
Phage protein contains sulfur — sulfur in cysteine and methionine — but no phosphorus.
Phage DNA contains phosphorus — phosphorus in the sugar-phosphate backbone — but essentially no sulfur.

So Hershey and Chase grew T2 phages in two batches: one labeled with radioactive sulfur-35, which got incorporated only into the protein, and one labeled with radioactive phosphorus-32, which got incorporated only into the DNA.
Two batches of phages, identical except for which part was labeled.

They infected fresh E. coli with one batch or the other, waited briefly, and then put the cultures in a kitchen blender — yes, an ordinary kitchen blender — to shear off whatever was stuck to the outside of the cells without damaging the cells themselves.
Then they spun the mixtures in a centrifuge to separate the cells, which pellet, from the loose stuff, which stays in the supernatant.
Then they asked: where did the radioactivity end up?
The result was clean.
In the sulfur-labeled batch — where the label was on the protein — the radioactivity ended up in the supernatant.
The protein had been on the outside, was sheared off by the blender, and stayed in solution.
In the phosphorus-labeled batch — where the label was on the DNA — the radioactivity ended up in the cell pellet.
The DNA had gone inside.
And critically, the cells that had DNA inside them went on to produce normal progeny phage.
The DNA had entered.
The protein had not.
And the DNA, alone, contained enough information to direct the production of complete new phages.
Therefore, the genetic material was DNA, not protein.
This was the experimental settlement of a question that had been open for decades.

Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty had pointed in the same direction with their 1944 transformation experiments in Streptococcus, but their finding had been resisted; many biochemists still believed protein, with its twenty amino acids and seemingly endless variability, was the more plausible carrier.
Hershey and Chase, with T2 phage and a kitchen blender, made the answer impossible to dodge.
That's the analogy I want you to hold for this segment.
Hershey-Chase is the experiment that proved DNA, not protein, carries the genetic information — and it was done in phage T2, in 1952, with a blender.

The whole shape of the next seventy years of biology — Watson and Crick the next year, the genetic code in the 1960s, recombinant DNA, sequencing, CRISPR — all of it follows from the moment when phage T2 made it clear that nucleic acid, not protein, was the heritable substance.
And it wasn't just the Hershey-Chase result.
The phage group worked out, in T-even phages and lambda, essentially the entire grammar of molecular biology.
The Luria-Delbrück fluctuation experiment of 1943, which showed that bacterial mutations are random rather than induced by the selective pressure — done with phage as the selector.
The first map of a viral genome — done in T4.
The discovery of mRNA — done by infecting E. coli with T2 and showing that a new, rapidly-turning-over RNA species appeared, encoding phage proteins.
The genetic code itself — cracked using mutations in T4.
The first synthetic gene — for a phage tRNA.
The first complete sequencing of a DNA genome — phage phi-X174 by Fred Sanger in 1977.
The first restriction enzymes — discovered as bacterial defenses against phage.
The list runs to dozens of foundational discoveries, all done on phage.
For a generation of biologists between 1940 and 1975, "doing molecular biology" largely meant "doing phage genetics.
Why was phage so productive a substrate?
A few reasons.
First, simplicity: a phage genome is small and tractable.
T4 has 300 genes, but lambda has only about 70, and phi-X174 has 11.
You can hope to understand the whole thing.
Second, fast generation times: a phage life cycle is minutes to hours, so you can run many experiments per day.
Third, large populations: you can easily grow ten to the eleventh phages in a flask, so rare mutants are not so rare.
Fourth, clean genetic selections: plaques on a lawn give you a single-particle readout, as we discussed.
Fifth, the host is also tractable: E. coli has its own deep genetic toolkit, and you can use host mutants to dissect what the phage needs from the host.
So phage was the right tool, at the right time, for the right question.

The molecular biology you know — the central dogma, the operon model, the genetic code — was largely worked out by people staring at plaques on petri dishes and asking what the phages were doing inside those clear holes.
